title: Classification of horizontal SL(2)'s
speaker: Colleen Robles
tags: Workshop on Fundamental Groups and Periods

proof_stages.label: Mumford-Tate-domain and horizontal-SL(2) setup
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes a connected real form acting on an open Mumford-Tate domain, recalls the induced Hodge grading on the Lie algebra, defines horizontal SL(2)s via Griffiths transversality, and sets the GR-conjugacy problem together with the associated real-split polarized mixed Hodge structure.

proof_stages.label: Bala-Carter-style representation-theoretic data
proof_stages.summary: Levi subalgebras are introduced as zero eigenspaces of integral semisimple elements, distinguished nilpotents are characterized as nilpotents whose minimal containing Levi is the ambient algebra, and the Bala-Carter dimension/parabolic tests are recalled.

proof_stages.label: Finite parameter set of allowed Levis
proof_stages.summary: After fixing a base point phi and a compact Cartan containing the Hodge grading element, the speaker defines the finite set of real Levi subalgebras whose semisimple part meets the stabilizer parabolic in a distinguished parabolic, then quotients it by the Weyl group of g^0_phi.

proof_stages.label: Classification bijection and normal representative
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem asserts a bijection between GR-conjugacy classes of horizontal SL(2)s and the Weyl-orbit set of allowed Levis, giving an explicit representative by a distinguished nilpotent, the projected grading element, and a formula for the Deligne splitting.

proof_stages.label: Computability and geometric meaning of the Levi
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains that the classification is algorithmic and interprets the Levi as the part of the ambient group that controls the degeneration, visible by decomposing a Hodge representation into Levi-modules and rotating pieces to obtain the Deligne splitting.

proof_stages.label: Forward proof: recovering the Levi from a horizontal SL(2)
proof_stages.summary: For a given horizontal SL(2), the associated mixed Hodge structure on the Lie algebra is split, its diagonal Deligne pieces form a natural Levi containing the standard triple, and a minimal conjugation- and phi-stable Levi is shown via Hodge-Tate classification to be one of the allowed Levis.

examples.name: Connected period-domain example with identical Hodge diamonds
examples.note: A period domain with six connected-real-form conjugacy classes of horizontal SL(2)s, two sharing the same Hodge diamond, illustrates that Hodge diamonds alone do not distinguish classes after passing to the connected automorphism group.
